speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number two with survivor Mr. Howard Chandler. You're working with your father now in the camp. Please continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we worked in these-- in retrospect, I think now because we had these ammunition factories, about 3,000-- approximately 3,000 Jews from our town were able to work there and, therefore, bide or buy our time, to-- for tomorrow, for tomorrow, hoping that things will improve and the war will end. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] We got, more or less, used to camp life. You-- there was no time for mourning. You know, you had to work and work hard. You had to account for yourself. Jews were expendable. We-- in the camp, there were only Jews. At work, we mixed with the general working population, which, of course, some of the Gentiles were friends with whom my father had dealings with. Amongst who, because he worked there before the expulsion, and because he was, more or less, able to foretell or maybe have some kind of inkling that things weren't always going to be like this, he gave some-- he gave to some of his friends or acquaintances or coworkers money, or maybe he gave them some valuables. I'm not so sure. And after the expulsion, when we weren't going-- able to go home again, although we were living in the same town, the vicinity, our accommodation was the-- the camp, which was, of course, surrounded by barbed wire and watchtowers with Ukrainian guards. You could only go and come under armed guard. And at work, since we were mixing with the general population, if you had friends there, it was a help. Some people were quite honorable. And my father was able to acquire from them, say, if he gave-- if he gave somebody, say, 100 to 200 zlotys. And money was of no value in particular, unless you were able to buy something or exchange it for-- say, some people bought you some apples. Some brought you some potatoes. Some brought you some bread. It helped. And we had an advantage, because we were local people. Knowing the local Christian population, it was a help. Of course, amongst the Jewish population in the camp, there were also people who were not native to our town. So they didn't know any of the Gentiles. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We also received occasionally-- not received, but they were given to my father through intermediaries in the camp-- sometimes a little parcel, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which helped out. Maybe some potatoes, some rice, whatever. Whatever they could part with. And this helped us. Of course, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] going back to the [INAUDIBLE] I mentioned that to you, when we were made to give up all our valuables. After being a while in the camp, and we-- we acquired a top bunk, which was the best bunk because it was always warmer there and there was nobody above you, I had my jacket hanging from whatever space there was between the top bunk and the ceiling of the barrack. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't think my father even knew. I-- I-- I-- I felt through my jacket, and I was suspicious. I opened it up. And I found 100 zloty note sewed in. If this guy at the [INAUDIBLE] Altov, his name was, Altov-- would have found it, he would have shot me. And I didn't know it was there. And another-- on another occasion, they had brought in some clothes to the camp to be distributed to the prisoners. I think this was one or one of two occasions where they had given us different coats. And I was given a-- a jacket, which was quite welcome, because I-- my clothes, like everybody else's, was almost in tatters. And some of the people were using cement bags to line their clothes in the winter time, particularly. We had to walk from camp to work-- I would imagine about two or three kilometers there and two or three kilometers back under-- under guard. And our shoes were getting worn, and our clothes was getting sprinkled with hot oil from the-- from the-- from the factory. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was given a jacket in-- in which there was a bullet hole in it, or two bullet holes, I forget now. And I found a one pound note in it, sewn in also in this jacket. And this pound note, I was able to go to Auschwitz with it and bribe a kapo there with it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, we were in this camp, Starachowice, and we worked there. Two epidemics broke out, where it took a heavy toll of people with typhoid fever. In our-- In the adjoining camp, on the [INAUDIBLE] camp, the Germans came one day and made a general appell, or roll call. And those who had typhoid fever-- I don't know whether you know how this-- this sickness evelop-- develops.

speaker: interviewee
text: You get a fever, And you hit the high of it. Either you die, or you start recovering. The problem was you had to go to work. And if you weren't going to work, you weren't getting enough food. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there were no nurses, no doctors, mind you. But there was two doctors-- one doctor in camp. What could he do for you? He had no medicine. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: The short and long of it was, is those who could go out-- that means those people who didn't contact the typhoid fever or those who were recovering and they were strong enough to go out on this roll call-- went out. They went into the barracks, and they shot everybody in the barracks who couldn't go out. And they made those outside walk, I mean, run around the perimeter of the camp. And those who fell behind, they shot. And they must have shot a few hundred prisoners there. And they were all buried right outside of the camp in a-- in a shallow grave. And a lot of the people weren't even dead, only wounded. And the Christians, the Poles, who were brought from the houses past this place, used to come. Even they had so much sympathy. They says, you wouldn't believe it. This place is going up and down. And they could see the blood coming out from the-- from the top of the shallow grave.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the earth was moving?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Anyway, so we expected them to come to our camp to do the same thing. My father was the first one to catch the typhoid fever, then my brother. I was the last one to catch it. So while they were sick, at least I could look after them, like hand them maybe some water or give them some food. Of course, they didn't want to eat. You needed medicine. Their mouths were getting so swollen, they couldn't open it. Anyway, my father pulled through, and I was going to work with a fever. I didn't want to stay in camp. I was afraid they'll come and shoot us. But they never turned up. So we were lucky in this regard. I don't know. They might have been afraid to come in and catch the-- catch the disease themselves. So quite a few people died. And those who made it, made it, and those who who didn't, didn't. And there was another problem there is with sanitation. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was-- there was no laundry. You couldn't wash your clothes or anything like this. And there were bed bugs and there were lice, and there were fleas. And a-- a friend of mine's father, Mr. Pajkus, he had a bottom bunk there. And a man was sick of typhus. And I could just see him there, still, like-- like today. And his whole body, with his clothing, was so full of lice that there wasn't a speck on the outside that you didn't see lice.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he just-- they must have eaten him up. They must have dried his blood up there. And, of course, everybody was so weak and so preoccupied with themselves, and that even if you tried to help him, there's-- what can you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first thing we used to do when we used to come home from work, before we used to go out-- outside in the camp, line up for our portion of soup, is take off your shirt and shake it out. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Otherwise you would be overcome with the vermin. In the summertime, as soon as they put the lights out in the barracks, the bed bugs would descend on you. And they are worse than the lice or the fleas. They just dry you. So sometimes we used to bribe the Ukrainian guards to let us sleep outside. And you could only do this in the summertime. You couldn't do it in the winter time. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There used to be selections in the camp. Occasionally, they would come first thing in the morning, while the people were coming home from the night shift, and look us over after making a roll call. And take out the weak ones, put them on a truck, take them to the next ravine or whatever, and then they shoot them. And they used to bring back, occasionally-- they used to bring people from other locations where they were no longer needed, and to replace these workers. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] If you were sick, there was a-- there was a barrack for a hospital. It was a one-way street. You go in there, and you come out like a bag of bones. They hardly feed you. The people were begging to be shot.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what kind of [INAUDIBLE] there was. They were shooting people for no reason. And these people they didn't shoot, because they were sick. The doctor attended them, but what could he do for them with no medicine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Occasionally, when some people were being accused of doing sabotage in the factory, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] or if somebody ran away, they would-- they would have reprisals. Most of the time, if you were accused of doing sabotage in the factory, you either sabotaged the machinery or you sabotaged the product, they would shoot you right at the station. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Sometimes, they would find out later who did it, or who they suspected. They would come to camp, lead you down the quarry there, and there was a place there where they used to make you dig your own grave and shoot you. But when somebody ran away, like I did on two occasions, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and if you weren't found within a specified time-- I don't know whether it was a day or two days-- they would take five people from either side of where you slept-- or if you have-- of if you had relatives, they would be right sleeping next to you-- they would take ten people and shoot them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Occasionally, the Judenrat, who were still in camp with us, were able to bribe the Germans and say, why would you take 10 productive people who were producing ammunition for the war effort and shoot them? Why don't you take out 10 people who are very sick from the sick barrack who are going to die anyway and shoot them? So occasionally, this was done. They would take down 10 people, make them dig their graves. And when they were finished, just shoot them. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Occasionally, we used to work outside of the perimeter of the factories. The factories were fenced in with, I don't know, about a 6 or 8 meter-- foot fence. We used to sometimes go out and repair the-- the railway lines or unload a wagon of some kind of product outside the factory, like road building materials or something like this. And on one such occasion, I could see the guard. He was a Ukrainian. He wasn't so watchful. And I knew the town, the surroundings, and I snuck away. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So the first thing you do is you run through town, make sure nobody recognizes you, because the local population wasn't above selling you out to the Germans for a pound of, or a kilo of, sugar and a bottle of vodka any time anybody found a Jewish person loose. That was the reward.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the reward, a kilo of sugar and a bottle of vodka. So I made sure nobody sees me. So I run in the back streets to the forests. And as I come to the edge of the forest, I said, OK. It was late afternoon already. What do I do now? So I thought, what do I do now? They'll take my father and my brother and eight other people and shoot them.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is still 1942?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is 19-- between 1942 and 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're 12, 13-- wait.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I was-- there were three boys in the camp my size that were able to-- to sneak through the net there. So I decided to go back. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I said, before I go back, I'm going to see Mrs. Glina, who was supposed to hide out my brother and who had a lot of our possessions. And I was going to confront her to ask her what's happened to my brother. This would be provocative, and I was afraid that she was going to call out the police to make a scene.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came in meekly. And I said, Mrs. Glina, I said, as you can see, I'm not having good clothes. And I remember that we left with you suits that will fit my brother. And we have-- you have shirts and you have linen, and you have this. How about giving me some to take back to camps? She says, what are you talking about? What are you talking about? I got nothing of yours.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was helping my father taking the stuff there, and I remember where she told us to put it. I kind of, you know, moved a little bit to the side. I went to the closet where we put the stuff. I took out a bolt of cloth, wrapped it around myself. And while this was taking place, her younger son, [INAUDIBLE], was my friend, came in. And as soon as he came in, she sent him out. And I was afraid she sent him for the police.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I took off with this bolt of cloth wrapped around me. I disappeared through the-- through the [INAUDIBLE], where they-- where they had the common [INAUDIBLE]. They had the common place where they used to take the cows. How do you say it? The commons. In English, it's the commons, where they-- where they-- where they fed the cows. I went through there, all the back ways I could, back to the factory. Of course, the-- the work-- the work party was not there anymore. And how do I get into the factories? It was all guarded. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So I went around and I waited for a gate to where the people are coming out for changes of shift. And as soon as they started walking away, I stayed in a place like that, and I joined them. And luckily, nobody saw me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went back to camp, and I had a bolt of cloth. And for this bolt of cloth, we gave it to some fellow who could sew and made-- I don't know what-- pants or a jacket. On another occasion happened the same thing, except it was a few months later. I could see the guard was not watching, you know. I took off. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And this time I went to the secretary of the lawyer, who was a good friend of my father, Mr. Szichowski, Jan Szichowski. I knocked on his door, and he looked at me like a ghost. He didn't know. He said, what are you doing here? He says, I didn't know you-- you were still alive. I said, no, I says, we're still alive. It's my father, myself, my older brother. We are in camp, and we're not doing too badly. And I says, Mr. Szichowski, I says, I ran away from camp and I think I'm going to go back. Could you give me some of the things that we left with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, of course, he says-- and I told him, I'm in a hurry to go back. So he took-- takes out a sheet of paper, like a lawyer. He says, sign here. I says, you give me what you want to give me, write it down, and then I'll sign. Even though I was that young, I knew already that signing a paper blank is not-- is not a very smart thing to do. He gave me I think two 100 zloty notes, and he gave me two or three shirts. I wrapped them around myself, and I went back to camp. The work party was not there anymore, and I didn't know what to do. And I decided to sneak back in, and the Ukrainian guard caught me. And this Ukrainian guard that was there, he was-- I think he had only one hand. His left-- something was wrong with his left hand. And he wasn't a bad fellow.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I started to cry in front of him. I says, look, we were working-- we were working here before. And the last time when you worked here, a lady who was a friend of ours, went by and they gave a couple of shirts to be laundered, and I have them with me. He searched me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I says-- he saw two white shirts, like I haven't seen white like this for a long time. Anyway, he gave me a good beating and let me go. He could have shot me there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then I went back to camp. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] We were there until July, I think it was, was in the summer time of 1944, where we started to dismantle the machines in the factory. We heard that the war wasn't go so-- going so good for the Germans anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they dismantled the big machines in the factory, and we loaded them on the trains. And then we were in camp and not going to work anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, of course, as time went by, we used to hear rumors that they were taking the people. Instead of taking them to the Ukraine, that they were being taken and gassed and burned. And, of course, nobody believed it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Or, I would say, the people that I knew didn't believe it. I said, why would they take healthy people that would work for them. We didn't fight against them. We didn't do any sabotage. We didn't break any laws. Why would they take people [INAUDIBLE] gas and burn them? [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] But I guess some people [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] believed. So while we weren't going to work, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] people-- like, say, a group of people-- would get together and decide, say, tomorrow night, we're going to break out. So this is what people did. They used to cut the wires and start running. So the guards shoot. Some people got wounded. Some people got killed. Some run away. The next night, the same thing. So they brought in more soldiers, more guards. They brought in heavy searchlights and heavy machine guns. And this thing was going on for about almost a week, I would say. Every night, despite people being shot, wounded, and they used to cut the wires and try and run away.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the sight was absolutely-- I don't know if you can believe it or not. What happened was, the people who got killed, they were dead, of course. The people who were wounded, they wouldn't let anybody go there and help them. And they were crying and moaning. They were begging to be shot. The-- the Germans wouldn't let anybody near them so as to discourage others from breaking out. Until one day, we were made to get out of the barracks, assemble. And the railway line wasn't too far away. They put us in a train. They closed it, and off we go. We didn't know where we-- where we would be-- be taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of train?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a freight train, closed freight train with four small windows, like two windows on either side, quite high. It was above my height. We were pushed on this train. It was standing room only. I had a knife with me [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and went with my father and with the other people in the train. And on the second or third day-- I don't know what, must have been on the second day-- everybody was naked. It was so hot. We didn't have food. Worse was we didn't have any water. And if you knew somebody had any water, you'd kill for it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we traveled on that train, I don't know, it seemed like an eternity. It must have been at least four or five days. And then one night we stopped in a station. And they shuffled. And we looked down. People stood on one another and looked out of the train. They said, who are you? Where are you coming from?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we said-- we were the same train, except they halved it. And they put us on two different railway tracks. And they said, well, I am standing on Dr. Kramarz. He was our Jewish physician in camp. He committed suicide, together with Walenzyk. I forget his first name. They both committed suicide.

speaker: interviewer
text: How? Do you know how?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a doctor. I guess he must have had some pills. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this-- and-- and-- as it was getting lighter, the train started. And at the crack of dawn, they opened the doors. And there were. I looked around. And although we were used to being in a camp with barbed wire, I never seen anything like it. They were double rows of electrified barbed wire, guards with dogs, people in funny, pajama-like uniforms. We were being yelled at and made to disembark. And when we opened the wagon, there were dead amongst us. We didn't know. We were all standing up. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You could stand three or four people-- people away from somebody, per-- person's dead. We-- once you go out, he fell. Of course, you knew these people. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we didn't know where we were. We'd never heard of Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's where we were, in Auschwitz, in Birkenau. We look in the front us, we see four big chimneys. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] After we got disembarked, and we were marched towards the direction of the chimneys. And I don't-- I didn't speak to my father. I didn't see anybody or remember anybody else speaking to anyone else. We just marched towards the place there. We figured, well-- you can imagine, we traveled for almost a week without food or water. We are so demoralized. You figure the sooner the end comes, the better. So you-- you won't have to suffer. And I think this was the time when people really realized all these rumors, or the so-called rumors that we heard, are right in front of us. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were made-- we-- we were put in empty barracks, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and was a muddy floor, and walls and roof. We were made to undress and being told that we'd put our clothing in orderly fashion, tie our boots and shoes, and so we can find them easily when we come out. And everybody did it. And un-- until today, I don't remember exchanging one word with my father or my brother. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were made to go into a place where, right away, you could see there was something strange. You see men and women naked in one-- in one place. Women cutting men's hair. Men's cutting women's hair. They weren't cutting the hair so much as tearing them out, because the clippers were dull. We were made to give everything up. Of course, we didn't have anything to give up. We were being searched. And we were told to go in to a place, and-- for [? entlousen, ?] you know, to-- to be cleansed. And we did as we were told. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we figured that's going to be the end. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Apparently, when we arrived in Auschwitz, we arrived there with a permit or with some instructions, because we were experienced ammunition workers. There was no selection. Those who were alive, dis-- alive, disembarked from the wagons. We were marched towards the-- the crematoria nearby, which was the [? entlousing ?] chambers, where we took the showers. There was no selection. Therefore, I would have never made it to Auschwitz, because I was too small. Because there was no selection, I made it through. And we were given a shower. We were [? entloused. ?] Then we went out. We were given some-- like this striped clothing. Of course, it didn't fit. Of course, this-- this wasn't-- didn't matter. We were again assembled and marched away, which was around the corner, through some camps. And we were assigned to go into the gypsy camp, the Zigeunerlager.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we stop there just for a minute?
